content:
First note that if we break the run of n ones into blocks of sizes \(k_1,k_2,\dots,k_m\), each block of size \(k\) contributes the repunit
\[
R_k=\underbrace{11\cdots1}_{k\text{ times}}=\frac{10^k-1}9.
\]
We want
\[
\sum_{i=1}^mR_{k_i}=1000
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\sum_{i=1}^m\frac{10^{k_i}-1}9=1000
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\sum_{i=1}^m(10^{k_i}-1)=9000.
\]
Since \(10^{k_i}-1\) can be \(9,99,999,\dots\) we set
\[
c_1\cdot9\;+\;c_2\cdot99\;+\;c_3\cdot999\;=\;9000,
\]
where \(c_d\) is the number of blocks of length \(d\).  Divide by 9:
\[
c_1+11c_2+111c_3=1000,
\]
with \(c_1,c_2,c_3\ge0\) integers.  One checks easily that \(c_3\) can run from 0 up to 9, and for each fixed \(c_3\) there is a certain range of \(c_2\), giving in all
\[
\sum_{t=0}^9\Bigl(\lfloor(1000-111t)/11\rfloor+1\Bigr)\;=\;460
\]
solutions \((c_1,c_2,c_3)\).  Each such solution yields
\[
n=c_1+2c_2+3c_3
\;=\;
1000-9c_2-108c_3
\;=\;
1000-9\bigl(c_2+12c_3\bigr).
\]
If we set \(u=c_2+12c_3\) then the admissible \((c_2,c_3)\) make \(u\) range over the union of intervals
\[
u\in[\,0,90]\cup[\,12,92]\cup\cdots\cup[\,96,106]\cup\{108\},
\]
which in fact is all integers \(0\) through \(106\), together with \(108\).  Hence there are
\[
107+1=108
\]
distinct values of \(u\), and therefore 108 distinct values of \(n\).  Thus
\[
a=108,
\]
and the sum of its digits is
\[
1+0+8=\boxed9.
\]
